Nari Kim (김나리)

Ph.D. Candidate, Second Language Studies

Michigan State University

I am a Ph.D. candidate in the Second Language Studies program at Michigan State University, advised by Dr. Peter I. De Costa. My research examines how students’ and teachers’ emotions and identities shape language learning and teaching in the age of AI, with a particular focus on building more inclusive language classrooms. My work draws on classroom-based qualitative inquiry, narrative approaches, and participatory design to address questions at the intersection of language education and human values.

|

Research

As a language learner, teacher, and teacher educator, I am curious about how students' and teachers' emotions and identities shape language learning and teaching in the age of AI. I am particularly interested in young language learners and the teachers who work with them.

My research primarily draws on participatory, ethnographic, narrative, and other qualitative approaches to investigate language learning and teaching. I also collaborate across methodological approaches, including quantitative and experimental research using tools such as eye-tracking. My dissertation brings these strands together through work with multilingual elementary students in language classrooms. See below for selected publications and presentations organized by research strand.

Question 01 Digital Literacies & Multiliteracies

How do language teachers and learners engage with AI and digital tools to support learning?

Exploring AI as a resource for language learning

Examining the biases, exclusions, ethical stakes, and power hierarchies embedded in AI

Investigating digital multimodal composing as a site of criticality and social action

Question 02 Emotions & Identity

How do emotions shape and get shaped by the contexts of language learning and teaching?

Investigating how learners' emotions influence language learning

Examining how teachers' emotions shape language teaching

Understanding the relationship between emotion, identity, and social context

Question 03 Inclusive ELT

How can English language education treat every learner's linguistic repertoire as an asset?

Questioning who defines "inclusion" and whose knowledge counts

Exploring diverse Englishes and translanguaging in classroom practice

Centering social justice and Global Englishes in English language education

Across all three strands Research–Practice Divide

Questions that come from classrooms, and go back to them.

Conducting classroom-based research grounded in real educational concerns

Using participatory design, ethnography, narrative inquiry, and systematic synthesis

Collaborating with teachers and returning research findings to classrooms and communities

Presentations

Timeline Journal Articles Book Chapters Book Review Under Review

Teaching

Teaching Philosophy

My teaching centers on helping students develop their identities as capable language learners and fostering a curiosity and passion for language that continues beyond the classroom. I design lessons that make room for risk-taking, treat mistakes as part of learning, and ground language use in authentic, contextualized work. I draw on an asset-based pedagogy that recognizes students’ linguistic and cultural backgrounds as resources rather than deficits, inviting their home languages, families, and ideas into the classroom.

I work with both current and future language teachers. As a practicing teacher licensed in Michigan, Alabama, and South Korea, I try to carry these commitments into my own classroom.

Language Teacher Education

Teach undergraduate courses in the TESOL minor at MSU that connect SLA theory with classroom practice, focusing on learner identity, investment, and equity in linguistically diverse settings.

Mentoring

Mentor doctoral students and early-career scholars on qualitative research methods, dissertation writing, and scholarly publication through coursework, guest lectures, and invited panels.

K-12 Practitioner Roots

Draw on a decade of experience in elementary multilingual and EFL education in my work in teacher education and research.

Current Teaching
Michigan State University
Department of Linguistics, Languages & Cultures
At MSU, I teach undergraduate courses in the TESOL minor that help future language educators connect second language acquisition theory to classroom practice in linguistically diverse settings.
LLT 307 Methods of Second and Foreign Language Teaching Instructor · Summer 2026

National and international approaches, methods, materials design, learner characteristics, and needs assessment in ESL and foreign language settings. Survey, evaluation, and application of major effective methods and materials.

LLT 361 Second and Foreign Language Learning Instructor · Summer 2025

Basic principles of learning a second or foreign language. Issues in first language acquisition. Theories of second language learning. Aptitude, motivation, attitude, learning grammar, age, learning in a classroom, myths, and facts about second language learning.

Student Feedback · LLT 361, Summer 2025

“I could tell that she really cared about teaching the material and knew a lot of information about it. She was very good at explaining concepts and being there for students.”

“Nari was super flexible and communicative. She also gave long, thorough feedback on each assignment that I really enjoyed reading and found very helpful.”

“She put so much effort into engaging with us. All the feedback she gave me was very helpful and thorough.”

“I have developed a much deeper knowledge of the theoretical basis of language learning and skills on making an inclusive lesson plan.”

K-12 Teaching Experience
10 years · English & General Education Teacher
Elementary Classroom Teacher, Multilingual Education Leader & Dispatched Teacher
Before entering graduate school, I served as a full-time English Language Teacher and General Education Teacher across three Daegu elementary schools for ten years — teaching EFL, serving as homeroom teacher, leading government-designated multilingual research programs, and directing cross-cultural initiatives for multilingual immigrant students.
Educational Academy for Boys & Girls
Volunteer Teacher
Columbus, OH, USA · Spring 2022

Supported teachers with English as a second language (ESL) instruction and materials development. Observed classes every Wednesday and Thursday from 8:30 to 11:30 throughout the entire semester.

Daegu Sehyeon Elementary School
Full-time English Language Teacher
Daegu, Republic of Korea · Mar 2021 – Aug 2021

Taught English as a foreign language (EFL) to elementary students before entering graduate school.

Daegu Education Training Institute
Department of Global Education · Dispatched Teacher (Competitively selected)
Daegu, Republic of Korea · Sep 2019 – Feb 2020
TESOL specialization6-month training program

Competitively selected as a leading English teacher by the Daegu Metropolitan Office of Education. Completed a 6-month intensive TESOL training program, focusing on learner-centered instruction and development of online/offline class materials.

Government Funding
$13,000  · Daegu Metropolitan Office of Education · 2019
Daegu Jukgok Elementary School
Full-time English Language Teacher
Daegu, Republic of Korea · Mar 2016 – Aug 2019  ·  Mar 2020 – Feb 2021
Elementary English Teacher Class Mentor
·Taught English to elementary EFL students across grades 3 to 6.
·Elementary English Teacher Class Mentor (2017–2021, competitively appointed): mentored in-service elementary English teachers through classroom observation, analysis, and professional feedback.
·Designed and implemented a maker education program that integrated language and literacy learning — using 3D pens to bridge hands-on creation with English communication skills.
·Facilitated English language learning through Robot English Drama, integrating programmable hamster robots and block-based coding (Entry) to engage students in collaborative, embodied storytelling in English.
·Integrated augmented reality (AR) into English lessons to create interactive, immersive classroom experiences that deepened student engagement with language content.
·Served as an instructional coach for in-service elementary English teachers: conducted systematic classroom observations, provided evidence-based feedback, and offered ongoing professional development support.
·Directed operations of the Daegu Character Education Center (Mar 2017 – Aug 2019): managed a district-wide program serving 2,000+ students annually from 21 schools, overseeing instructor recruitment, program planning, budgeting, scheduling, and official documentation.
·Daegu–Australia Telecollaboration Research Group (Mar 2016 – Aug 2021): participated in global telecollaboration research with Southmoor School, Australia. MOU signed between Daegu City Office of Education and Victoria Southwestern Office of Education, involving 23 classes across 19 Daegu schools.

Service

Service Philosophy

I believe service is an essential part of sustaining a collaborative and supportive field. My service work includes reviewing scholarship, mentoring graduate students, organizing academic events, and offering invited teaching and workshops. Through these efforts, I aim to support accessible and supportive field.

Peer & Conference Review

Manuscript review across six journals, with abstract review for the TESOL Convention and AERA since 2023.

Invited Talks & Workshops

Panels at Michigan State University on K-12 research partnership, qualitative research practices, and graduate-school life.

Leadership & Community

Symposium organizing volunteer, KERIS content reviewer, and an active member of the SLS graduate community.

Professional Service

Scholarly Review

Knowledge-Sharing

Invited Talks & Workshops

I use invited panels and student sessions to translate research methods and graduate-school experience into practical guidance for peers and newer students at Michigan State University.

  • 2025 · SLS Anniversary Homecoming: Panel on creating K-12 research spaces through relationship building and intentional timing, in Doing research in K-12 school contexts (Organizer: C. Cinaglia).
  • 2025 · SOSLAP Graduate Student Organization: Invited panelist, “Advice for QRPs and Thesis.”
  • 2024 · SOSLAP Graduate Student Organization: Invited panelist, “Surviving Graduate School.”

Institutional Service

Association & Program Work

I contribute through the program structures that make scholarly exchange possible, helping organize events and host visiting scholars who connect the wider field.

  • Symposium organizing volunteer: supported logistics and on-site coordination for the 2025 Graduate Student Symposium on Engaged Multilingual Pedagogy and Intersectionality.
  • Invited speakers hosted: Ofelia García (CUNY Graduate Center) and Jon Wargo (University of Michigan).
  • Field visibility: represented the Second Language Studies graduate community across program and conference-facing events.

Community Engagement

The Language Pod

I contributed to public-facing language science at the Language Pod, the COSI (Center of Science and Industry) lab devoted to the study and communication of language.

  • Public science communication: explained language research to visitors and led interactive demonstrations about the science of language.
  • Research outreach: supported participant recruitment for ongoing language research studies.
